coredata.dc:description: This study investigates the photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue (MB) dye using a novel ZnO/shrimp shell-derived carbon quantum dots (SS-CQDs) composite under UV light irradiation. The ZnO, SS-CQDs, and their composite were synthesized via a hydrothermal process and extensively characterized through X-ray diffraction (XRD), UV–visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), scanning electron microscopy with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (SEM-EDX), elemental mapping, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. TEM analysis provided detailed insights into the morphology and particle size of the composite, confirming the uniform distribution of SS-CQDs on the ZnO surface. The incorporation of SS-CQDs into the ZnO matrix was found to reduce the composite's band gap, significantly enhancing its photocatalytic activity. Using a low catalyst dosage of 20 mg in a 10 ppm MB solution, the ZnO/SS-CQDs composite demonstrated a remarkable degradation efficiency of 94 % within 90 min under UV light irradiation, following a pseudo-first-order kinetic model equation with a rate constant of K = 0.0278 min−1. These findings highlight the potential of the ZnO/SS-CQDs composite as an efficient and sustainable material for photocatalytic dye degradation, offering a promising solution for environmental remediation and wastewater treatment.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study investigates the photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue (MB) dye using a novel ZnO/shrimp shell-derived carbon quantum dots (SS-CQDs) composite under UV light irradiation. The ZnO, SS-CQDs, and their composite were synthesized via a hydrothermal process and extensively characterized through X-ray diffraction (XRD), UV–visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), scanning electron microscopy with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (SEM-EDX), elemental mapping, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. TEM analysis provided detailed insights into the morphology and particle size of the composite, confirming the uniform distribution of SS-CQDs on the ZnO surface. The incorporation of SS-CQDs into the ZnO matrix was found to reduce the composite's band gap, significantly enhancing its photocatalytic activity. Using a low catalyst dosage of 20 mg in a 10 ppm MB solution, the ZnO/SS-CQDs composite demonstrated a remarkable degradation efficiency of 94 % within 90 min under UV light irradiation, following a pseudo-first-order kinetic model equation with a rate constant of K = 0.0278 min−1. These findings highlight the potential of the ZnO/SS-CQDs composite as an efficient and sustainable material for photocatalytic dye degradation, offering a promising solution for environmental remediation and wastewater treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, concerns about the harmful impacts of environmental damage, especially water pollution, have been increasing. These issues not only jeopardize public health and well-being but also hinder economic growth. Therefore, it is essential to urgently develop and implement effective strategies to tackle these environmental challenges, ensuring the long-term sustainability of our society [1–3]. Water contamination is mainly caused by the discharge of dyes into water bodies from industries such as tanneries, food processing, textiles, and paper manufacturing [4–6]. These dyes increase the toxicity and carcinogenic potential of water, posing serious health risks to humans and animals that consume it [5,7]. Health issues linked to water contamination include nausea, vomiting, eye irritation, diarrhea, allergies, and environmental damage like stunted plant growth, reduced photosynthesis, disruption of aquatic ecosystems, and bioaccumulation [8]. For instance, wastewater containing methylene blue dye (MB) can lead to severe health effects such as jaundice, vomiting, cyanosis, and an accelerated heartbeat [9]. Therefore, removing harmful dyes from water is crucial to ensure its safety for consumption. Several water purification methods are employed, including sedimentation, boiling, distillation, disinfection, filtration, chemical treatments (such as precipitation, coagulation, and adsorption), and catalytic processes [8,10]. Among these, photocatalytic methods have gained significant attention due to their advantages, including reusability, chemical-free operation, cost-effectiveness, simplicity, and effectiveness in treating low-concentration of pollutants [11–13]. Photocatalysis is an environmentally friendly and highly efficient technique that converts organic pollutants into harmless substances (CO2 and H2O) without producing additional pollutants, making it a promising solution for water purification [14,15]. The combination of carbon materials with inorganic functional components enhances the performance and properties of these materials [16]. Thus, the development of inorganic-organic hybrid materials with well-defined structures is crucial, as it could lead to the discovery of new, and more efficient photocatalytic materials [17].

The hybridization of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with various materials such as TiO2, SiO2, Fe2O3, Ag3PO4, Cu2O, and CdS has been widely documented in the literature [15,18–20]. Zinc oxide (ZnO), a prominent group II–VI semiconductor with a broad band gap of 3.37 eV, is particularly appealing due to its excellent photocatalytic properties, low cost, abundant availability, and ease of preparation in both bulk and nanostructured forms [21]. ZnO is extensively studied for its potential in eliminating environmental pollutants from wastewater, polluted air, and contaminated water. However, its large band gap limits its photocatalytic activity to ultraviolet (UV) light, which is a major drawback, as only about 5 % of sunlight is captured by undoped ZnO [22,23]. Enhancing the photocatalytic activity of ZnO under visible light presents a significant challenge for practical applications. Shifting the optical response of ZnO to the visible spectrum could greatly improve its efficiency in photocatalysis [24,25]. Therefore, considerable research is focused on reducing the excitation threshold energy to capture a larger portion of visible light for conversion into photochemical energy. Various approaches, such as doping with metal and non-metal elements and forming heterostructures, are used to extend the absorption of ZnO into the visible range [26].

ZnO/CQD nanocomposites have gained significant attention for their potential in pollutant degradation, with recent advancements in their development [27–31]. However, despite the growing research on CQDs across various applications, limited studies have explored the synthesis of ZnO/CQD composites specifically aimed at improving the photocatalytic performance of ZnO under visible light [27,32].

This study presents a novel approach that integrates photocatalysis, biomass waste utilization, and environmental remediation to promote sustainability. The CQDs were derived from shrimp shell biowaste using an environmentally friendly hydrothermal method. This process offers a sustainable pathway for converting organic waste into valuable nanomaterials. The synthesized CQDs were then incorporated into ZnO to form the ZnO/SS-CQDs nanocomposite. This combination leverages the synergistic effects of both components, resulting in enhanced photocatalytic efficiency for the degradation of methylene blue via photo-oxidation. The nanocomposite demonstrated improved light absorption and reduced electron-hole recombination. Overall, this work highlights the ZnO/SS-CQDs nanocomposite as a high-performance material for organic pollutant degradation, emphasizing its dual benefit for both environmental remediation and sustainable resource utilization.

Zinc acetate dihydrate (Zn(C2H3O2)2·2H2O, 99.5 %) was obtained from Sisco Research Laboratory (SRL) Pvt. Ltd., while hydrazine hydrate (50–60 %) and methylene blue were sourced from Merck, India. All chemicals and reagents were used as received without any additional purification. Distilled water (DI water, 18.2 MΩ cm) was prepared using the Millipore ultrapure water system.

The shrimp shells were collected from Chennai fish market, Tamil Nadu, India, and processed to synthesize highly luminous carbon dots using a single-step hydrothermal approach. The shrimp shells were washed several times before they were suspended in to 450 mL of the ethanol-water solution in a 1:3 ratio and then mechanically agitated at 70 RPM for 24 h. The shells were then dried in a drying oven at 70 °C to yield 1 g of powdered material. The powder was mixed with 50 mL of deionized water and stirred for 3 h until the suspension was uniform. The mixture was loaded into a Teflon-lined, 110 mL stainless steel autoclave and hydrothermally treated at 200 °C for 18 h in a hot air oven. In such a process, the shell-decomposed components efficiently converted into intensely photoluminescence carbon quantum dots. The as-prepared SS-CQDs were therefore kept at 4 °C for stabilization and further use (Fig. 1 a).

The synthesis of ZnO nanorods using the hydrothermal method was carried out with precise control to ensure the formation of high-quality nanomaterials. First, 2.18 g of zinc acetate dihydrate (Zn(C2H3O2)2·2H2O) was dissolved in 60 mL of distilled water, with rapid stirring to ensure complete dissolution. Next, 4 mL of hydrazine hydrate was added dropwise to the zinc acetate solution under continuous stirring, a critical step to initiate the synthesis process. The mixture was then transferred to a Teflon-lined reaction vessel, creating a sealed hydrothermal environment. The reaction proceeded at 155 °C for 24 h, facilitating the nucleation and growth of ZnO nanorods. After the reaction, the resulting white precipitate, consisting of ZnO nanorods, was thoroughly washed several times with deionized water and ethanol to remove any impurities or unreacted substances. Finally, the ZnO nanorods were freeze-dried at −84 °C, preserving their structure and yielding dry samples ready for further characterization and potential applications.

The previously synthesized ZnO was dispersed in a solution of pre-prepared SS-CQDs, and the mixture was stirred at room temperature for 1.5 h to ensure adequate interaction between ZnO and SS-CQDs. The resulting solution was then subjected to a hydrothermal reaction in an autoclave at 110 °C for 4 h, promoting the formation of the ZnO/SS-CQDs composite. After the reaction, the product was washed and centrifuged at 7000 rpm to remove any impurities and unreacted materials, ensuring the purity of the final product. The composite was then freeze-dried at −84 °C to yield the final ZnO/SS-CQDs samples, which exhibited enhanced structural purity, making them suitable for advanced photocatalytic applications.

The crystalline structure of the ZnO/SS-CQDs composite was analyzed using X-ray diffraction (XRD) in the 20°–80° range. XRD measurements were performed with a D8-Advance X-ray diffractometer (BRUKER, Mannheim, Germany), utilizing Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å). The instrument was operated at 40 kV and 15 mA, with data collected at a scan rate of 10°/min. To assess the optical properties of the ZnO/SS-CQDs, UV–visible spectra were recorded using a UV-Jasco spectrophotometer, providing valuable insights into the optical characteristics of the synthesized material. Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was conducted on the samples using an ALPHA II spectrophotometer (Bruker, Germany). The morphological features, including the shape and size of the ZnO/SS-CQDs, were examined using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM). A dispersion of the ZnO/SS-CQDs was prepared by casting it onto aluminum foil, which was then dried on a hot plate. The sample was further dried overnight in a hot air oven at 50 °C to ensure complete drying. FE-SEM imaging was performed using a CARL ZEISS – SIGMA 300 FE-SEM equipped with EDAX (Germany), operating at an accelerating voltage of 1.10 kV.

The photocatalytic performance of the ZnO/SS-CQDs nanocomposite was assessed by investigating the degradation of MB dye under the UV–Visible light irradiation. The light source was a 300 W Xe lamp that was equipped with a UV cut-off filter (wavelength >420 nm), served as the light source. In the experiment, 20 mg of the ZnO/SS-CQDs photocatalyst was dispersed in 100 mL of an aqueous solution containing 10 ppm of MB dye. The mixture was stirred for 15 min to establish adsorption-desorption equilibrium before exposure to UV light. At regular intervals (every 15 min), the photocatalyst was separated from the solution by centrifuging the samples. The remaining MB concentration was measured by recording the absorbance at the peak wavelength (λ max) of 663 nm using a UV–visible spectrophotometer. The degradation efficiency (%) was calculated using the following formula:

The electrochemical properties (Mott-Schottky (M-S) plots) of the synthesized catalysts were analyzed using a CHI 760E electrochemical workstation from CH Instruments (Austin, TX, USA). These measurements were conducted in a standard three-electrode setup, where the catalyst-coated glassy carbon electrode (GCE) served as the working electrode, an Ag/AgCl electrode acted as the reference electrode, and a Pt wire electrode was used as the counter electrode. The electrolyte used was a 0.1 M Na2SO4 solution. To prepare the working electrode, 1 mg of the catalyst was dispersed in 5 mL of a 1 % Nafion solution, which was prepared by diluting commercial Nafion with a 1:1 mixture of deionized water and ethanol. The mixture was then sonicated for 30 min to form a homogeneous slurry. Subsequently, 4 μL of the slurry was applied to the surface of a GCE with a 3 mm diameter, and the electrode was left to dry at room temperature for 1 h, forming a stable catalytic film. The Mott-Schottky (M-S) plots revealed the semiconducting properties and band structure of the materials, confirming that ZnO and its composites exhibit n-type behavior. This finding underscores their potential for photocatalytic applications, with the flat-band potential providing insight into their energy alignment and promoting efficient charge transfer processes.

The photocatalytic degradation experiment was carried and every measurement was accurately recorded to ensure accuracy. However, to measure catalyst stability, the ZnO/SS-CQDs composite was subjected to five successive degradation cycles, which determined the efficiency after each cycle to assure consistency as well as reliability. The degradation efficiency was calculated using (Eq. (1)). The reaction kinetics followed a pseudo-first-order model, expressed as ln(C/C0) = - Kt. where k is the rate constant (min−1), obtained from the slope of the linear regression plot of ln(C0/Ct) vs. time. All measurements were performed using UV–Vis spectrophotometry. The data analysis, including graphical representation and kinetic evaluation, was conducted using Origin software. Moreover, additional advantages and limitations have been acknowledged and discussed in the discussion section at the appropriate places.

The powder XRD patterns of both bare ZnO and the ZnO/SS-CQDs composite exhibit distinct peaks corresponding to the crystalline structure of ZnO, predominantly in the wurtzite phase. In the XRD pattern of bare ZnO, characteristic diffraction peaks are observed at 2θ values of 31.68°, 34.35°, 36.09°, 47.36°, 56.48°, 62.70°, 66.23°, 67.87°, 68.99°, and 76.77°. These peaks are assigned to the (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), (200), (112), (201), and (202) crystallographic planes, respectively, which are consistent with the hexagonal wurtzite structure of ZnO (JCPDS 36–1451) [32]. However, a slight shift in the XRD peaks is observed upon incorporating SS-CQDs into ZnO, which can be attributed to lattice expansion and strain effects. The SS-CQDs may induce tensile strain or intercalate within the ZnO lattice, leading to an increase in the interplanar spacing (d-spacing). According to Bragg's Law (nλ = 2dsinθ), this expansion causes a decrease in the diffraction angle (2θ), resulting in a shift of the peaks toward lower angles. This shift indicates that the crystal structure of ZnO has been modified due to the presence of SS-CQDs. Overall, the XRD analysis provides valuable information on the crystalline characteristics and structural properties of both bare ZnO and the ZnO/SS-CQDs composite (Fig. 2 a), highlighting their potential applications in optoelectronics, photocatalysis, and sensors. The Scherrer equation was used to estimate the average crystallite size of the synthesized nanocomposite.

In this equation, 'k' represents the shape factor (0.94), 'λ' is the wavelength of Cu Kα radiation (1.54184 Å), 'D' denotes the mean crystallite size in nanometers (nm), 'β' is the full-width at half maximum (FWHM) of the diffraction peaks in radians, and 'θ' is the Bragg angle.

Equation (3) represents the Uniform Deformation Model (UDM) in the Williamson-Hall (WH) approach, which assumes isotropic strain in all directions of the crystallite. This method is essential for assessing microstrain in the crystallites, which significantly impact the material's quality and structure. It also helps to determine crystallite sizes with minimal strain effects. As shown in Fig. 2b, the WH plots, the Scherrer method indicated a crystallite size of ∼141 nm. The discrepancy between the values suggests the presence of tensile microstrain (ε) of 1.24 E−3. In contrast, the Scherrer method yields a larger crystallite size of 141 nm (Table S1), while the WH method estimates a smaller size of 28.1 nm (Table S2), indicating a tensile microstrain (ε) of 0.00124 (Tables S2 and S3). A positive slope value in a WH plot indicates the presence of tensile strain in a material [33]. The variation in crystallite size can be attributed to the effects of microstrain, which is influenced by synthesis conditions such as hydrothermal and calcination temperatures, processing time, crystallite size, crystal defects, and oxygen vacancy concentration. A strong correlation exists between crystallite size and microstrain. Previous research had demonstrated an exponential increase in microstrain as crystallite size decreases, especially when the size is below 5 nm [34,35]. In the case of ZnO/CQDs, the observed increase in crystallite size is accompanied by a slight decrease in microstrain. This is likely due to the formation of a stable organic capping layer of SS-CQDs around the nanoparticles (NPs). The capping layer enhances the uniformity of the NPs but restricts the full relaxation of stress, resulting in a reduction of strain. Furthermore, the capping layer may influence the interactions between the NPs. In contrast, for ZnO/SS-CQDs, the higher strain and smaller crystallite size are associated with specific synthesis conditions [32,34,36].

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of SS-CQDs, bare ZnO, and the ZnO/SS-CQDs composite provide valuable insights into their optical characteristics and electronic transitions, highlighting the unique behaviors of each material as well as the synergistic effects when combined. In the SS-CQDs spectrum (Fig. 3 a), distinct absorption peaks are observed in the UV range of 270 nm. These peaks are attributed to π -π∗ transitions and surface states within the carbon nanomaterials. The π-π∗ transitions are associated with the aromatic regions of the carbon structure, indicating the presence of conjugated π systems. Surface states arise from functional groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and other oxygen-containing groups attached to the SS-CQDs, which create localized energy states that interact with the π -π∗ transitions, enhancing the absorption features [37]. The sharp absorption edges observed in the SS-CQDs spectrum suggest quantum confinement effects, which result from the reduced size of the SS-CQDs, leading to discrete energy levels and size-dependent optical properties.

In the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of bare ZnO (Fig. 3b), a distinct absorption edge is observed around 387 nm, which corresponds to the bandgap energy of ZnO. This edge marks the initiation of direct band-to-band transitions, where electrons are excited from the valence band to the conduction band. The bandgap energy of ZnO, approximately 3.3 eV, is responsible for its characteristic absorption in the UV range [38]. The absorption spectrum of the ZnO/SS-CQDs composite (Fig. 3c) displays characteristics peaks from both ZnO and SS-CQDs, highlighting the combined optical properties of the two materials. Compared to the individual components, the composite spectrum showed broadened or shifted absorption features, suggesting the interactions between ZnO and SS-CQDs. These interactions are likely due to the formation of heterojunctions at the interface, which promote charge transfer processes. The broadening of the absorption peaks indicates an increased density of states at the interface, resulting in new electronic states that enhance the composite's light-harvesting efficiency [39]. Additionally, new absorption peaks may appear in the composite spectrum, indicating the formation of charge transfer-modified electronic states at the interface between ZnO and SS-CQDs [40–42]. These peaks could result from the hybridization of electronic states from both materials, forming new molecular orbitals that span the interface. Such charge transfer processes are vital for photocatalytic and photovoltaic applications, where efficient separation and transport of photo-generated charge carriers are critical for high-performance devices [43].

The Tauc plot is an important method for determining the optical band gap of materials, based on the Tauc equation [44]:
(4)

where Eg is the optical band gap. For SS-CQDs, the Tauc plot indicates a band gap of approximately 5.1 eV, characteristic of quantum dots due to quantum confinement effects. In contrast, ZnO shows a band gap of about 3.3 eV, reflecting its intrinsic properties. The ZnO/SS-CQDs composite exhibits a reduced band gap of approximately 3.1 eV. This reduction results from synergistic interactions between ZnO and SS-CQDs, including charge transfer at the interface and an increased density of states. The heterojunctions at the ZnO/SS-CQDs interface allow hybridization of electronic states, forming new molecular orbitals that span the interface and lower the band gap (Figs. S1a–c).

The broad absorption range of the composite, extending from the UV to visible regions, enhances its photocatalytic activity by increasing electron-hole pair generation under light irradiation. Surface functional groups on CQDs further modified the electronic structure, contributing to the reduced band gap of composite.

The photoluminescence (PL) properties of the synthesized SS-CQDs were systematically investigated under varying excitation wavelengths. The PL spectra exhibited distinctive emission peaks that were dependent on the excitation wavelength, a behaviour characteristic of conventional carbon nanomaterials as the excitation wavelength increased from 250 to 400 nm in 10 nm increments. The PL spectra of SS-CQDs, depicted in Fig. 4 (a–c), offer essential insights into their optical characteristics and excitation-dependent emission behaviour, which are crucial for their use in photocatalysis, especially when combined with metal oxides like ZnO. Fig. 4a presents both the excitation and emission spectra of SS-CQDs. The excitation spectrum peaks around 360 nm, indicating the optimal excitation wavelength for maximum emission, while the emission spectrum peaks around 440–460 nm [45]. In Fig. 4b, the PL emission spectra are shown for excitation wavelengths from 250 nm to 400 nm. The observed red-shift again confirms the excitation-dependent emission characteristics of SS-CQDs, suggesting a heterogeneous size distribution and varied surface chemistry that led to multiple recombination pathways for electron-hole pairs. Fig. 4c illustrates the PL emission spectra of SS-CQDs under excitation wavelengths from 250 nm to 360 nm. As the excitation wavelength increases, the emission peak shifts to longer wavelengths, indicating multiple emissive sites within the SS-CQDs. This red-shift points to quantum confinement effects and surface functional groups, with broad and tuneable emission profiles peaking around 430–450 nm. The significant Stokes shift observed helps to reduce the re-absorption of emitted light, thus enhancing fluorescence efficiency. The strong and tuneable fluorescence of SS-CQDs makes them ideal for boosting the photocatalytic activity of metal oxide composites like ZnO/SS-CQDs. SS-CQDs act as photosensitizers, extending light absorption into the visible spectrum and facilitating charge transfer processes. This reduces electron-hole pair recombination and improves photocatalytic efficiency. The interaction between SS-CQDs and ZnO forms heterojunctions, enhancing charge carrier dynamics and extending photocatalytic activity to visible light.

Fig. 5 displays the FTIR spectra of SS-CQDs, bare ZnO, and the ZnO/SS-CQDs nanocomposite, highlighting key spectral features and chemical functionalities. In the spectra, a prominent peak observed at 3388 cm−1 is attributed to the hydroxyl (O–H) group, [46] characteristic of surface hydroxyl groups. This peak indicates the presence of surface functional groups contributing to the hydrophilicity and surface chemistry of the materials. Another significant band at 1628 cm−1 is assigned to the CC stretching vibrations of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons present in the carbon quantum dots. This peak reflects the presence of aromatic carbon structures within the SS-CQDs, contributing to their optical and electronic properties [32].

In the spectrum of the ZnO/SS-CQDs nanocomposite, the emergence of a new peak near 652 cm−1 is observed, attributed to the Zn–O–C [47]. This peak indicates the formation of chemical bonds between ZnO and carbon quantum dots within the composite structure, likely arising from the surface interactions or chemical coupling between the two materials. The band at 1260 cm−1 is linked to the C–O–C stretching due to the deformation of the furan ring. Meanwhile, the appearance of a new band at 1338–1345 cm−1 in the ZnO/SS-CQDs spectrum suggests the formation of a Zn–COO- group caused by the interaction between SS-CQDs and the ZnO carrier [32,48,49]. Various functional groups present in the SS-CQDs and ZnO/SS-CQDs, such as hydroxyl and carbonyl groups, can play a significant role in photocatalytic activity. These groups aid in the absorption of light and the generation of reactive species, enhancing the photocatalytic performance of the composite. The FTIR analysis thus confirms the successful synthesis and integration of the ZnO/SS-CQDs composite (Fig. 5), laying the foundation for its application in photocatalysis.

SEM and TEM analysis of the ZnO/SS-CQDs composite revealed rod-shaped particles with an average size of 200 nm (Fig. 6 a–d). The elongated structures, indicative of rod-shaped particles, exhibited uniformity and consistency across the material. Rod-shaped nanoparticles offer enhanced properties, such as increased surface area and improved mechanical strength, making them promising for various applications. EDX analysis of the ZnO/SS-CQDs composite reveals the presence of three main elements: zinc (Zn), oxygen (O), and carbon (C). The weight percentage of these elements is as follows: Zn (51.49 %), O (30.38 %), and C (17.68 %). The significant presence of zinc and oxygen confirms the primary composition of ZnO in the composite (Fig. 7 a). Additionally, the presence of carbon indicates the successful incorporation of SS-CQDs into the ZnO matrix. The relatively high carbon content (17.68 %) compared to oxygen (30.38 %) suggests a substantial loading of SS-CQDs, which is essential for improving the photocatalytic efficiency of the composite. The integration of SS-CQDs into the ZnO structure is expected to enhance the light absorption range, facilitating more effective charge transfer processes and reducing electron-hole recombination rates.

Next, the elemental mapping analysis provides a visual representation of the spatial distribution of the elements within the ZnO/SS-CQDs composite. The mapping shows (Fig. 7b–e) a uniform distribution of zinc, oxygen, and carbon across the composite, indicating a well-dispersed integration of SS-CQDs within the ZnO matrix. The homogeneous distribution of carbon suggests that the SS-CQDs are evenly dispersed, which is critical for the formation of effective heterojunctions between ZnO and SS-CQDs. These heterojunctions play a vital role in enhancing the photocatalytic activity by promoting efficient charge separation and transfer.

The optical properties of the synthesized SS-CQDs were meticulously examined using the Commission International De l’Eclairage (CIE) Chromaticity Diagram, providing essential insights into their colorimetric attributes crucial for optoelectronics and photonics applications. Under excitation at 360 nm, the SS-CQDs demonstrated an emission peak at 440 nm, falling within the blue spectrum (Fig. S2), indicating their potential for blue light-emitting devices [50]. The precise chromaticity coordinates (x, y) were determined to be (0.15, 0.11), aligning with the blue region on the CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram (Fig. S3), affirming the purity of blue emission a desirable trait for various applications, including display technologies and bio-imaging. The efficacy of SS-CQDs in enhancing the photocatalytic activity of ZnO was evaluated through the synthesis of ZnO/SS-CQDs nanocomposite aimed at improving the degradation of methylene blue dye under visible light irradiation. Integrating SS-CQDs into ZnO extended its optical absorption into the visible range, mitigating ZnO's inherent limitation of large band gap absorption mainly restricted to UV–visible spectrum. This enhancement, corroborated by the CIE chromaticity diagram analysis, attributed the blue emission from SS-CQDs to the improved visible light absorption of the ZnO/SS-CQDs nanocomposite. The effective generation of ROS upon exposure to visible light facilitated by SS-CQDs contributed significantly to the degradation of organic pollutants like methylene blue, further enhancing the photocatalytic efficiency of the nanocomposite.

The degradation efficiency and kinetic properties were assessed to elucidate the contribution of these materials to the enhancement of the degradation process. Fig. 9 illustrates the time-dependent degradation of MB dye, represented as C/C0, among the three photocatalytic systems. Pure SS-CQDs (Fig. 9a) achieved a degradation efficiency of approximately 16 % after 90 min, whereas the light-only condition (absence of catalyst) yielded negligible degradation of 2 %. ZnO (Fig. 9b) exhibited a reduction, attaining a 35 % degradation efficiency owing to its inherent photocatalytic characteristics. The ZnO/SS-CQDs composite (Fig. 9c) demonstrated the most pronounced reduction in C/C0, achieving 94 % degradation in the same duration, thereby affirming its remarkable efficacy in degrading MB.

Kinetic analysis (Fig. 10 a–c) revealed that the degradation process follows pseudo-first-order kinetics, as indicated by the linearity of the plots. The plots of ln(C/C0) = - Kt versus time for all systems yield linear relationships, from which the rate constants (k) were determined. The calculated rate constant (k) for SS-CQDs was found to be K = 0.00186 min−1, indicating their restricted performance (Fig. 10a). ZnO demonstrated a higher rate constant of K = 0.00472 min−1 (Fig. 10b), suggesting a moderate level of efficiency in its catalytic performance. The ZnO/SS-CQDs composite exhibited an impressive rate constant of K = 0.0278 min−1 (Fig. 10c), which is nearly 15 times greater than that of SS-CQDs and 6 times greater than ZnO (Table 1 ). This significant enhancement can be attributed to the efficient charge transfer and reduced recombination facilitated by the SS-CQDs in the composite. These factors extend the lifetime of photogenerated carriers, thereby increasing the production of reactive oxygen species and improving the degradation efficiency, as shown in Fig. 11 a-d.

Overall, the experiment was conducted only once; however, the composite underwent five consecutive cycles to assess its catalytic stability. The photocatalytic degradation efficiency of the ZnO/SS-CQDs composite across these five cycles is presented in Fig. 12 . The degrading efficiency of the composite is 94 % in the first cycle, whereas it decreases to 86 % for the fifth cycles. This decrease in degradation efficiency over subsequent cycles can be attributed to a variety of factors, such as the accumulation of residues on the catalyst surface, partial leaching of the active material, and potential structural changes in the catalyst after repeated use.

Due to the involvement of centrifugation or washing after the dye degradation process, a small quantity of the photocatalyst is lost during each cycle. Additionally, the catalyst's overall efficacy may be diminished by the accumulation of organic metabolites resulting from the degradation of MB. Although the cleansing and recovery processes assist in alleviating this issue, residual residues or dyes may persist on the catalyst's surface, obstructing active sites and thereby reducing its efficiency. Furthermore, the photocatalyst's morphology or surface properties may alter gradually as a result of repeated exposure to UV–visible light. Despite the fact that the ZnO/SS-CQDs composite exhibits high stability, these subtle modifications may result in a reduction in photocatalytic efficiency over subsequent cycles. Nevertheless, the composite maintains 86 % of its initial activity after five cycles, despite the minor decrease in degradation efficiency. This demonstrates outstanding reusability and overall stability for practical photocatalytic applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanistic pathway of photocatalytic degradation process
ce:section.ce:para:
The degradation of MB dye using ZnO/SS-CQDs composites follows a comprehended mechanism involving electron-hole pair generation and ROS production. This process is essential in the degradation of dyes, as the conduction band, valence band, and flat band potentials of the materials involved dictate the overall performance in this context [52]. The parameters obtained from the Mott-Schottky (M-S) plot play a crucial role in the production of ROS, which are key to breaking down the pollutant. The flat band potential values derived from the Mott-Schottky (M-S) plots (Fig. S4) for ZnO and ZnO/SS-CQDs are 0.10 V (vs Ag/AgCl) and 0.05 V (vs Ag/AgCl), respectively. The following equation can be used to convert these values to the NHE scale [53,54].

The photocatalytic degradation of MB dye using ZnO/SS-CQDs composite comprises a variety of complicated reactions that occur under UV–visible light irradiation (Fig. 13 ). The fundamental process is the formation of ROS, which play an important role in the degradation of organic pollutants such as MB [55]. The catalyst absorbs photons, which excites electrons from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB), resulting in electron-hole pairs. UV–visible light is absorbed by the excited ZnO or ZnO/SS-CQDs composite when the photon energy is greater than or equal to the band gap energy. UV light provides sufficient photon energy for ZnO, which has an optical band gap of 3.3 eV. In contrast, the ZnO/SS-CQDs composite shows a decreased band gap of about 3.1 eV, which increases its activity under visible light. As shown in Equation (14), for excitation, the photon's energy must be greater than the band gap (E g).
(14)
E

≥ E

The electrons are promoted from the VB to the CB after the photon energy is absorbed, resulting in the formation of holes in the VB of the catalyst (Equation (15)) [56].

The generation of electron-hole pairs occurs after activation. However, the photocatalytic efficiency can be decreased by the recombination of these couples [57]. The CQDs in the ZnO/SS-CQDs composite help to reduce recombination by receiving electrons from ZnO's CB, enabling holes in the VB of ZnO to stay and participate in the oxidation processes. Equation (16) provide an illustration of this procedure.

The electrons in the CB are transferred to oxygen molecules adsorbed on the surface of the catalyst, where they reduce O2 to form superoxide radicals (O2 −). This reaction is shown in Equation (17).

Simultaneously, the holes in the valence band (VB) of the catalyst interact with water molecules or hydroxide ions, resulting in the formation of hydroxyl radicals (•OH). These highly reactive radicals are essential for breaking down dye molecules. The relevant reactions are outlined in Equations (18) and (19). Additionally, excited electrons are prevented from recombining with the holes in ZnO, as shown in Equation (20). This suppression of electron-hole recombination is vital for enhancing photocatalytic efficiency, as it ensures a higher availability of charge carriers for redox reactions. The electrons transferred to the SS-CQDs are subsequently used to reduce molecular oxygen in the solution, producing reactive superoxide radicals (•O2 −), as illustrated in Equation (20).

The ROS generated, including superoxide anions (O2 −) and hydroxyl radicals (•OH), play a crucial role in the degradation of MB dye. Hydroxyl radicals are particularly effective in targeting the chromophore of the MB dye, breaking it down into smaller, less toxic molecules such as CO2 and H2O, as shown in Equations (21) and (22). Therefore, the ZnO/SS-CQDs composite not only enhances charge separation but also possesses a reduced band gap (approximately 3.1 eV), which allows it to better harness visible light, increasing its efficiency under UV–Visible spectrum. The generation of ROS and their subsequent attack on the MB dye molecules by superoxide anions (O2 −) and hydroxyl radicals (•OH) result in the complete mineralization of the dye into harmless byproducts like CO2 and H2O. The ZnO/SS-CQDs nanocomposite shows excellent photocatalytic activity, which allows for 94 % degradation of methylene blue under UV light irradiation. Such a nanocomposite was synthesized using an eco-friendly and cost-effective method derived from biomass waste. This material is promising for environmental remediation due to its reduced electron-hole recombination and visible-light absorption. However, its prospect for widespread implementation is limited by the difficulties associated with scalable production. After the fifth cycle, reusability decreases gradually and leads to a reduction in efficiency. Further work will be carried out by tuning the band gap, increasing the active surface area, and creating heterojunctions which further minimize electron-hole recombination, enhancing stability, and broaden its applicability toward real-world wastewater treatment.

The ZnO/SS-CQDs nanocomposite was prepared through a straightforward hydrothermal method which showcased outstanding photocatalytic performance. Extensive characterizations were confirmed the successful integration of SS-CQDs into the ZnO framework, effectively expanding its light absorption into the visible range and minimizing electron-hole recombination. When evaluated with a catalyst dosage of 20 mg in a 10 ppm MB solution, the composite achieved an impressive 94 % degradation efficiency within 90 min under UV light exposure, adhering to a pseudo-first-order kinetic model with a rate constant of (K) 0.0278 min−1. These results highlight the potential of ZnO/SS-CQDs composite for efficient photocatalytic dye degradation. Additionally, utilizing catalysts derived from biomass waste provides an environmentally friendly and cost-effective method for wastewater treatment. This study demonstrated the promise of ZnO/SS-CQDs composite as effective material for environmental remediation, offering a sustainable and practical approach to eliminate organic pollutants from water. The remarkable performance achieved underscores the potential for their application in diverse photocatalytic processes.
